channel.title: Proposals And Policy (new threads)
channel.description: Threads in the forum category "Proposals And Policy" - What can we do to improve the site? Ask any questions you may have regarding site structure and policy.

item.title: Question re: Editing others' articles
item.description: May we fix on-site links formatted as external links?
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I hope I've found the right place for site rules questions. My question's about this rule (emphasis mine):

Editing others' articles: You may correct grammar, spelling, or formatting errors on other peoples' articles. Please put a summary of changes in the 'description of changes' box. Any further edits require permission from the original author or Rewrite team.

Often, an on-site link (triple brackets) is formatted as an external link (single brackets). As the Google Chrome users among you may already know, these external links don't work for some users while they are logged in (because we visit scp-wiki.wikidot.com instead of scpwiki.com as a login workaround; visiting any link formatted as https://scpwiki.com/whatever gives us an error message1). Clicking a link that ought to be an on-site link and receiving an error is unpleasant, as is manually replacing scpwiki with scp-wiki.wikidot in the address bar.

I don't know if using the 'wrong' link type is technically a formatting error, but I am hoping to receive mod/admin confirmation that it falls under this category.2 Changing these links' format type would not alter the article text, it would just make the links work properly for those of us who have to use scp-wiki.wikidot.com instead of scpwiki.com for technical reasons.

Getting author permission each and every time, just to change link formatting type from external to on-site, would be hugely annoying for both the editor and the author. I am hoping that we readers can be allowed to make these formatting changes without obtaining author permission each time.

So, tl;dr: If a link is formatted as an external link, but would work properly as an on-site link, may we go ahead and make that formatting change without bothering the author for their permission?

Footnotes
1. Note that the error only appears when an external link specifies https. It's some sort of certificate recognition error. Links formatted as on-site links work fine for us Chrome users, because wikidot helpfully inserts whichever base address the reader is already using.
2. I actually created an account because I wanted to fix various minor errors as I came across them while reading.

item.title: Could you swap the locations of 'delete' and 'link'?
item.description: They are too close, please do something to not to accidentally press on the wrong button, so please make its use more convenient.
item.content:encoded:
I am afraid if I'll accidentally press 'delete' rather than 'edit' since they are a bit too close to each other.
So could you please do something about this little problem?

item.title: Collapsable side panel on all browser sizes
item.description: The navigation panel on the left side of the page should have a button to collapse it out of the way on the desktop/tablet version of the site
item.content:encoded:
Description: Wikidot uses adaptive scaling for its UI elements. There is a navigation panel on the left side of the site that, when the screen is less than 768 pixels1, collapses off screen with a button to bring it in and out. I request that this panel have be collapsible regardless of window size (although it would be open by default for medium-large screens). An example of this behavior can be found here.

If this is not possible, a simpler option would be to change the 768 breakpoint to a larger value. For this I conjecture that 1500 pixels would be approximately correct.

Reason: The side panel does not adapt well to tablet devices. When using a tablet device in portrait mode, the panel is stuck taking up about a third of the screen, while providing little value. This, in my opinion, dramatically harms the reading experience. This problem is easily remedied for all devices by making the panel collapsible.

In the event that this is not feasible, I suggest a breakpoint of 1500 pixels based on my knowledge of common device sizes. Once you step down from a desktop monitor, devices are almost exclusively tablets and smartphones2. On either of those devices, the panel should be hidden by default, regardless of their horizontal resolution. For this reason, I suggest a value that is only a little lower than the standard horizontal resolution on a modern device, while still allowing for some padding for DPI scaled devices, such as laptops. This solution has weird edge cases, and could easily cause problems for other device sizes, so I don't recommend it unless there is no better option.

Advantages:

  • Improved readability for tablet devices, and other large portrait screens (some desktop setups use a "sideways" monitor for reading).
  • More economical use of screen space

Disadvantages:

  • Slight increase in UI "clutter", as a new button would need to be added
  • Increases required clicks by 1 for some navigation tasks on some browsers
Footnotes
1. This is the breakpoint listed in the CSS. In practice, the value will change based on various scaling settings of the device, because nothing is ever simple.
2. When considering actual hardware resolution. Laptops considerably complicate this problem because they frequently use DPI scaling, making them appear lower resolution than they actually are. My 1080p laptop "looks" like it has a horizontal resolution of 1650 pixels, because of DPI scaling.

item.title: Accessible Issue
item.description: I’m sure this seems stupid to old veterans, but Id like to be heard out
item.content:encoded:
Hello,

So the SCP Foundation is an incredible premise that can create some unique stories based on it’s premise alone. That being said, as someone who has now made multiple attempts at SCP’s I’d like to talk about some issues I see in the sight as an outsider.

+ Greenlight Troubles
- Greenlight Troubles

The site’s process for creating new SCPs, especially for new creators, is close focused at best. The fictional Foundation is supposed to be a clinical world facing fantastical elements, but as someone coming in, the similarities to the real site are a bit unnerving. To say nothing of the users, from personal experience they are fine, and sometimes friendly, this is purely about how a brand new author. To give credit, yes an author can develop ideas outside the site plenty, but that doesn't change they then have to convince at least two reviewers in a small paragraph there idea is worth it. Not impossible, obviously, but a little convoluted. I think the ability for more freedom in trying to convey what makes an idea worth it could benefit this site greatly. Once again: this spot isn’t impossible, but cam present real problems to someone in trying to cram an idea into a small format.

+ Aaaand its gone
- Aaaand its gone

The rating system means with -10 likes, a page meets the criteria for deletion. Now again, I can appreciate the benefits of running a strict site, but something about this seems a little cruel. For a site requiring hard work from new creators, all it takes is ten people deciding to press a button saying they didn’t like it, and that says to the author this page is worthy of deletion. It could be absolutely certain that if every site had a shallow enough criteria, many beloved posts would be gone before they blew up. Im not saying enough downvotes isn’t indicative of a post possibly needing deletion, but maybe that particular field needs to be stretched, and authors in general given a chance. After all theres no way to actually know if the majority of site users dislike an SCP, the votes are only indicative of the people who found that page.

+ Slightly Personal
- slightly personal

Once again, I’m sure to some this seems like the ravings of a writer who was a little hurt, and in some part I give credit that it does come from that. More so though, I’d argue that this is also the perspective of someone who has worked hard to try and join really creative people, and been turned away not necessarily by them, but by a system coming across as super insular. It occurs to me maybe I’m also ridiculous for trying to join a catalogue cresting the 6000’s, but under the impression that this universe could be even more I did wanna try. Definitely some fault is mine in writing, lets not pretend any old SCP’s I made would be the next 096, but my point lies in that: in trying to add to something I’ve seen as fun and a little silly, I’ve been met with an incredibly high wall, in some ways earned by the site, but in many ways just as ridiculous.

In closing: the site’s a little too insular. This doesn’t necessarily warrant a whole overhaul, but maybe a reflective look at how some things should be run. I suppose if you want to write this off as a newbee venting, thats fine, but I think it would great benefit the Foundation to genuinely look at how it handles new ideas, and what kind of criteria it has, and whether or not its always fair.

item.title: Questions about SCP Content on Twitch.tv and more
item.description: I want to create a Twitch Channel based on a SCP Article
item.content:encoded:
Hey! I recently came up with an idea for a SCP Article and Concept that I don't think has ever been done before and isn't really covered in the wiki's legal guidelines.

Essentially I want to create a VTuber (Virtual YouTuber or Twitch Streamer) to stream on Twitch with. VTuber's are generally 3D models that are rigged to react to the operator's movements and emotions. I wanted to create one based off of a SCP article (That i am currently writing) which would be useed as his backstory.

For more information on the Concept: VTuber SCP Concept

So…. I have a lot of questions…

+ show block
– hide block

Questions about Twitch Licensing and Credits:
1. Since the Character I would be streaming as is based on a SCP Article which falls under SCPwiki’s CC Licensing, I would assume the content I produce would need to fall under that same License? If so, I'm not sure how to set that up with twitch as I'm not entirely sure what their content falls under.

2. With the Character being an original creation of mine, would I still need to link to it’s article page and the Author (Me) for credit? Or would just linking to the SCPwiki be sufficient?

3. What rules and/or limitations (if any) would I need to abide by in my content since twitch is an Offsite platform? Examples: 1. Would I be able to stream myself as this character playing a game? even though the game may fall under a different license than CC? 2. As a musician, would I be able to cover copyrighted songs? (provided I follow twitch’s guidelines concerning music as well)

Questions about the Model:
I created the Model and Name before I had the idea to make it a SCP. In that case, would the model (including related images) and name still belong to me, but just be used on the wiki and on stream with my permission? Or would the model and name need to be under CC as well?

Not all of the content being producing would be directly related to the SCP universe, but if the character is connected, I'm not sure what limitations and restrictions would be applied

I love the SCP Universe and don't want to do anything that would be against their guidelines, nor do I want to incur the wrath of a DMCA Take down.

In closing I have no idea what I am doing lol, and any legal help or advice is extremely appreciated!

(There are probably many things that I have not thought of and many questions may rise up. if you have any questions or suggestions of other questions I should be asking, please let me know)

item.title: Individual Rating Modules for Log Entries
item.description: Perhaps an easier way to quality-control.
item.content:encoded:
Low-quality content naturally piles up in places like 682's termination log, 1459's extended logs, and other very large collaborative lists. Inevitably, a page curator has to go through and prune away the bad entries. I imagine that this is tiresome and time-consuming for the curator, and it also means that garbage entries in these logs live much longer than an equally garbage SCP would. If there were some way to install individual rating modules for each item on the log, so they could be voted on individually, then perhaps that might save some time for the curator and shorten the lifespan of the bad entries.

I thought that the log of extranormal events or unexplained locations was already like this, but it seems that I was mistaken.

item.title: Link to "Top Rated Pages" in sidebar?
item.content:encoded:
The sidebar, under "Discover Content", has a link to "Top Rated New Pages", but there is no link to "Top Rated Pages": http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/top-rated-pages

For new readers, a link to the top rated pages of all time seems like a great place to start.

Is there a particular reason why there isn't a link to it in the sidebar?

If not, could an admin please add one, under Discover Content?

Thanks!

item.title: Why has the Heritage Collection died?
item.description: Can we continue doing that at all?
item.content:encoded:
I've seen the Heritage Collection and I always wonder-

Why did they not add any SCPs after December of 2013?

We got to continue doing that, its a good way to show the best SCPs on the site.

Contact me if this thread has been moved.

item.title: New fairer voting system
item.content:encoded:
I feel a NEW voting system based on upvotes-per-view would stop good and nostalgic but not awesome ancient articles from being so very dramatically advantaged on top lists, while dramatically good new articles can't ever hope to catch up over time due to the very existence of the "http://www.scpwiki.com/top-rated-pages" page unless the reader really wants to read more than 1000 articles.

We need a system like this one.
http://www.kittenwar.com/
You can tell the "losingest" kittens are not there just because they are new, and the "winningest" kittens aren't there because they are old, this system does a better job of ranking quality than the current system.

The system would require:
-Knowing which SCP numbers are not a 404 or empty page or administrative issues page.

-The reader is shown 2 article links, they open in a new tabs. The first is fully random (to be fair), and the other is bias toward articles that showed up less in the NEW voting system 20 percent of the time, and fully random the rest of the time.

-The reader is asked after reading both articles to return to the tab of origin and pick which one they prefer.

-The best articles will win more "SCPfights".

-If you don't do it, some extrernal site will and will have ads on top of the "rate my SCP!" voting thingy.

Thoughts?

item.title: SCP Fodder List
item.description: A user-generated series of links to interesting and strange things that might help get the creative juices flowing.
item.content:encoded:
I rather like the idea of an ever-expanding list of links to resources for authors to look into, in order to create or come up with articles, as well as to draw the community closer together.

The list of links could be anything, from strange political ideas like Posadism, cults like Raelism, to the rantings of people like Francis E. Dec, to natural phenomena like the migrations of birds or water's unique properties.

I think it would help with bringing the community closer together as well, as people would be able to find others with similar interests in particular bits of real life lore.

item.title: Separate SCP Sections
item.description: As the title says, I think this site should make separate sections for SCPs
item.content:encoded:
As the summary as the title says… that XP. I don't know about what everyone thinks on the whole since I'm fairly new, but I think we should make separate sections for SCPs for people making new ones around here. What I mean is, we should make like one section for Humanoid/Organic SCPs, one for Inanimate objects such as the rings or the teddybear SCP (173 would also count in this category), and ones for abstract/spacetime SCPs, such as the stairwell SCP or ones involving portals and dimensions.

Why am I suggesting this? Well, several reasons. 1, it would make it easier to organize what ideas/drafts are posted in the forums, 2, people looking into ideas/drafts of a specific subject can have an easier time finding what they're interested in, and 3, people critiquing/greenlighting the works can find the work they're actually interested in reading about. Otherwise you could be spending a whole lot of wasted time looking under a dozen SCPs that sound like one thing, only to discover its a "rock" scp or something, you know?

Just a suggestion to help the site with its searching and all that. What do you guys think could help the site?

item.title: Out-of-mainlist hub where fictional characters are SCP-fied?
item.content:encoded:
What I'm proposing is essentially a separate section of the site, like the -J and -EX skips, where users will be able to submit SCP-fied fictional characters. Say, someone may want to make a file on Pennywise. As per site polilicy, a straight-up inclusion of his character cannot be made a mainlist SCP. They also can't (probably) rewrite him into something different, as was done with Chtulu. With this additional hub though, they can just turn him into SCP form.

Additions to this hub will have to be submitted into the Ideas Forum, be greenlighted and then go through the Draft Forum as well, to ensure their quality. I assume that an additional critics team may also be needed, to ensure that no low-effort posts are made (it's easy to write slopy conprocs and an uninteresting file and assume that just "cause it's Godzilla people will like it").

Edit: Because the main issue here seems to (understandably) be the legal complications, I thought I'd leave these links here. I'm absolutely not an expert and if people with legal experience say that it can't be done, it can't. But :

  • I think that a "parody" of a copyrighted character is considered Fair Use.
  • An SCP article presenting a known, existing character as a prisoner in a Site would most likely be considered a Fan Fiction or transformative work. Both cases are considered to be fair use.
  • There is legal precedent of fanfictions being recognized (transformative works)

Edit 2: Argument debunked, too many legal issues.

item.title: Organise the Anomalous Items Log
item.content:encoded:
I propose that entries in the Log of Extranormal Events, Log Of Anomalous Items, and Log of Unexplained Locations be given some level of organization (similar to the 914 Experiment Log), primarily so entries in these pages can be referred to elsewhere on the wiki.

For example, consider the following entry in the Log of Extranormal Events:

Event Description: A small canoe in the Mississippi River was consumed whole by a Carcharocles megalodon, along with its two occupants. Carcharocles megalodon has been extinct for 2.6 million years, and the river in question is much too shallow to contain a creature of that size.
Date of Occurrence: 02/14/2018
Location: Undisclosed location on the Mississippi River, USA.
Follow-up Actions Taken: Amnestics were administered to witnesses, and a cover story was circulated claiming the canoe occupants were intoxicated and capsized their vessel accidentally. A task force was sent to locate and capture the anomalous entity, but all attempts to locate the specimen failed.

If this interests me, and I want to refer to it in an SCP or tale, it is practically impossible to do so. Of course I can go ahead and do it any way but I cannot refer to say, "Extranormal Event 536" because there is no designation given to each entry, and thus no way for a reader to find the entry I am referring to.

In fact, I am only writing this post because this situation has happened to me more than once (I have had an idea for incorporating an entry from one of these pages into something I'm writing or an idea I'm working on but haven't been able to since there is no way to reference it).

This change would be relatively easy to do and is warranted seeing how this is a collaborative writing project and these pages are featured on the very front page. (I'm aware that entries in the Unexplained Locations Log are given designations but this is a bit pointless seeing how they aren't organized in any meaningful way and are still impossible to find for a reader if they are referenced elsewhere without using ctrl+f)

And while we're at it, maybe split them into some sections with collapsibles.

Edit: Just to clarify in case anyone gets the wrong idea, I'm not asking that entries be given their own anchors or anything like that, just some designation that allows them to be referred to elsewhere on the site.

item.title: Hubs for top rated SCPs and top rated GoI formats?
item.content:encoded:
In addition to a hub for top rated pages (everything on the site), there's a hub for just top rated tales. Why not also hubs for top rated SCPs and top rated GoI formats? From looking at the source code for top rated tales it's very simple and self updating once created.

And if this does get done, maybe there should be separate hubs for joke SCPs and non-joke SCPs.

item.title: Hub for story hubs?
item.description: Because we didn't have enough story hubs hubs. (no, seriously.)
item.content:encoded:
Hello there. I am a new member of the SCP Foundation site, and have Big Ideas™. But before I can do these things, I want to help other people such as newcomers to this site.

One of my biggest issuse with the site is that , though it is very good site, it isn't as organized as some would want. The biggest contender (in my opinion) for that problem facts that there are so many hubs and they are all over the place. So many people have created their own stories and people have found them once, and lost them because they could not find the and areas for these collections of writing.

As an example, a new user could find a story, let's call it Anomalous Trash Cans. It's a comedy, that the user enjoys very much. The next day, the user tries to find the Anomalous Trash Cans hub, but cannot find it as there are many articles under a similar name. If only there were a way…

So I have an interesting proposal. A hub of all hubs , one which would hold hubs for series, (like the Antimemetics Division or our "Anomalous Trash Cans") with a link in either the "Info Pages" menu, or the "Backgrounds" menu.

But clearly this is a lot of work, so of course there has to be a good reason for this. In recent times, though the Foundation has been growing in size, I have seen a small outage in the creativity of our community. Some of us are, in some strange way, confined to writing only articles, tales, or canons and have lost our creative spark. If somebody wants to make an SCP story series, then they should bloody well do it then, and not have it lost in the tumbling gumjumble of hubs with two feet of dust on them. But they should also know where other story hubs are for their own reading and inspiration.

So basically, a hub for story hubs. (I apologise for those cognitivly injured by my own stupidity if I messed up the formatting and/or knocked down your house with an information flood.)

Thank you for listening to my TedTalk.

item.title: Should SCP-999 be rewritten?
item.description: Just wanted to start a thread to propose that we rewrite SCP-999, due to its age and the new CG5 song.
item.content:encoded: Just wanted to start a thread to propose that we rewrite SCP-999. Due to its age its writing is rather weak compared to how good its concept is. The new song by CG5 will be bringing more attention to it and the tale "New Job" so I just wanted to ask, should it be rewritten to include details from said tale in the style of other unique SCPs.

item.title: Can I delete my own forums messages?
item.content:encoded:
Tried to delete one of my forums comments on a per page discussion thread yesterday, but the site said I needed admin/moderator permissions to do that.

I'm not sure if that's a glitch or something, or if it applies to other areas of the site, or other wikis.

Anyways, couldn't find any information on this, so I decided to ask.

EDIT:
+I just tried to delete this message, and got the following popup:
-I'm confused as to what's going on here

item.title: Small Error in Butterfly Squad Roster page.
item.content:encoded: Under the 6th bullet point on Guidelines for Contacting Us on the Butterfly Squad Roster page, it says: "All your draft are critique by us." I believe it should say: "All your drafts are critiqued by us" I doubt this was intentional.

item.title: German CC Licenses on the English Wiki
item.content:encoded:
I found this image that I would like to use that is licensed under "Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Germany," and wanted to verify whether or not such licenses are compatible with the English SCP Wiki's image policy.

Moreover, are there similar types of "foreign" licenses out there, and if so, how do they interact with the English Wiki's policies? I gleaned what I could from the Dummy's Guide to Licenses, but want to be completely sure that I'm not stepping on anyone's toes, so to speak.

item.title: Contest Forum
item.content:encoded:
Contests on this site are always interesting because they people in a competitive spirit and bring out creativity by providing constraints. Seeing the exquisite corpse contest was actually what prompted me to make an account here.

Unfortunately, there aren't a lot of contests run here, and I wouldn't want to ask our hardworking staff to do more than they already do, so I propose we create a forum section for it. This way the community could hold contests itself. All we would need is a stickied thread with an outline of requirements for a contest (Deadline, topic, prize (if any), people running it, word limit, etc.). We could even use this to have more detail on the staff-run contests and save some space on the main page.

In short, I think having a contest forum would be beneficial and take up little to no resources, so I hope you consider it.
